serving.description: 1 platter (approximately 250 g including rice and side salad)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cutlet platter serving is approximately 250 g, comprising a breaded fried cutlet (around 120 g), steamed white rice (around 100 g), and a small side salad or shredded cabbage (around 30 g).

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Food Composition Database — Cutlet Platter (G00010735)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database record G00010735 for Cutlet Platter. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several micronutrients not reported in the HPB source; set to -1 where unavailable.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a composite cutlet platter. GI estimated at 65 based on the dominant carbohydrate component: white rice (GI ~72) and panko breadcrumbs (GI ~60), weighted by approximate carbohydrate contribution. The blended estimate of 65 places this dish in the medium GI category. GL per serving calculated as: (65 × 4.35 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 250 g serving) / 100 = approximately 7.1 g. Source: University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries for white rice and breadcrumbs.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 35 mg per 100 g; potassium 152 mg per 100 g provides a favourable Na:K ratio
hypertension.guidance: The cutlet platter as recorded has very low sodium (35 mg/100 g); however, patients with hypertension should request tonkatsu sauce or gravy on the side, as these condiments can add 200–400 mg sodium per serving and substantially raise the meal's sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate GI (~65) from white rice and panko breadcrumbs; dietary fibre is 0 g per 100 g
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should substitute white rice with brown rice or reduce the rice portion, and request a baked or air-fried cutlet to lower the overall glycaemic load of the meal from approximately 7 g to under 5 g per serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying generates AGEs and increases total fat; saturated fat content not reported but likely elevated from frying oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit deep-fried cutlet consumption to occasional meals, request baked or grilled preparation to reduce saturated and trans fat exposure, and avoid high-sodium sauces to protect endothelial function.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried cutlet preparations; request baked or grilled cutlet to minimise AGE formation, and add anti-inflammatory vegetables such as broccoli or leafy greens to the platter.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A Cutlet Platter is a Japanese-influenced dish popular in Singapore and across Southeast Asia, consisting of a breaded and deep-fried meat cutlet — most commonly pork loin (tonkatsu) or chicken breast (chicken katsu) — served on a platter alongside steamed white rice and shredded cabbage or a small side salad. The cutlet is coated in panko breadcrumbs, which produce a distinctively light, crispy, and airy crust with a juicy, tender interior. The dish is typically accompanied by tonkatsu sauce (a thick, sweet-savoury Worcestershire-style sauce) or Japanese curry. Per 100 g, the HPB Singapore database records 43 kcal, 2.81 g protein, 1.61 g fat, and 4.35 g carbohydrate, with very low sodium at 35 mg. The low recorded energy density reflects the composite nature of the dish including rice and salad components. Dietary fibre is 0 g per 100 g, which is nutritionally relevant for glycaemic management. The dish carries a moderate glycaemic index (~65) due to white rice and panko breadcrumbs. Clinically, the primary concerns are the deep-frying method (AGE generation, elevated saturated fat), the absence of dietary fibre, and the potential for high sodium from condiments. It is suitable for most diets in moderate portions but requires modification for patients managing type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis.
common_user_queries: Is cutlet platter suitable for diabetics?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (G00010735), an approved primary regional source. The record is marked as reviewed with confidence 0.95 in the input. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for white rice and panko breadcrumbs, clearly documented.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these fields are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database record G00010735 for Cutlet Platter as of 2026-04-16. No supplementary approved source was available to fill these gaps without risking misattribution. 2. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB source G00010735; the composite dish as recorded reports no dietary fibre. 3. Food group corrected from input value 'Grains and staples' to 'Meat and alternatives' to accurately reflect the dish identity. 4. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for white rice (GI ~72) and panko breadcrumbs (GI ~60); no direct GI measurement for a composite cutlet platter was found in approved sources. 5. Halal status set to false as pork is a primary ingredient in the canonical tonkatsu preparation; chicken katsu variants may be halal-certified — consumers should verify with the specific vendor.
